coredata.dc:title: Optimized nitrogen-doping of carbon quantum dots from Banana Peel waste: A highly selective Fe2+ sensor probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Siva Kumar, Harivalagan
Shamsudin, Siti Aisyah
Ahmad Azian, Muhammad Nasrullah
coredata.dc:description: Abstract This research explores into the eco-friendly synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) utilizing discarded banana peel waste as a sustainable carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen dopant. The study aims on the critical gap in environmentally sustainable ion detection system by optimizing the fluorescent properties of CQDs for Fe2+ detection applications through nitrogen doping at an optimum ratio. Herein, we reported a facile one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize NCQDs with varying nitrogen dopant concentrations. The precursor material was characterized by thermal gravimetric analysis and Raman spectroscopy which exhibited a more amorphous carbon structure. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and Proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy proves the successful bonding of nitrogen functional group to pristine CQDs. The obtained nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) exhibited precise size control, with an average size of 5.05 nm. Optical analysis showed that NCQDs with a nitrogen doping level of 1.25 % had the best photostability, brightness, and fluorescence stability. These NCQDs demonstrated high selectivity in detecting Fe2+ through a fluorescent quenching mechanism. These findings suggested that obtained NCQDs has potential as environmentally friendly fluorescence probes in the application water filtration systems and medical diagnostics, particularly in Fe2+ detection and sensing due to their high selectivity and efficient fluorescent properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence probes
Banana peel waste
Metal ion detection
Green synthesis
Biomass-waste

Selectivity of CQDs towards metal ions
Possible mechanism of NCQDs-1.25 % detecting Fe2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of NCQDs using citric acid and banana peel waste and ethyelendiamine as starting materials.
The degradation decomposition of banana peel in oxidative environments.
The Raman spectra of banana peel powder and the cellulose fibre representing amorphous and crystalline structure.
(a) TEM image of CQDs, (b) TEM image of NCQDs-1.25 %, (c) The size distribution histogram of the prepared NCQDs-1.25 % and (d) The size distribution histogram of the prepared CQDs.
FTIR spectra of NCQDs-1.25 % compared to CQDs.
(a) H1NMR spectra of CQDs. (b) H1NMR spectra of NCQDs-1.25 %.
(a) XPS spectrum of NCQDs-1.25 % (b) XPS spectrum of C1s region of NCQDs-1.25 %.
(c) XPS spectrum of O1s region of NCQDs-1.25 % (d) XPS spectrum of N1s region of NCQDs-1.25 %.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, NCQDs-1.25 %, NCQDs-5 %, NCQDs-10 % and NCQDs-15 % at the range of 250 to 600 nm. (b) FL emission spectra of NCQDs-1.25 %, NCQDs-5 %, NCQDs-10 % and NCQDs-15 % at the fixed excitation wavelength of 280 nm.
(a) Tauc plot of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 %. (b) Schematic illustration of energy levels (HOMO and LUMO) of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 %.
Comparison of fluorescence decay profiles of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 %.
(a) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution curve of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 % (b) Zeta potential (mV) curve of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 %.
(a) I/I0 value of NCQDs-1.25 % diversed with metal ions at 2 mM each (b) Photographs of NCQDs-1.25 % diversed with metal ions at 2 mM each under 345 nm UV light.
(a) Comparison I/I0 value of NCQDs-1.25 % and CQDs with Fe2+ ions at 2 mM each (b) Photographs of NCQDs-1.25 % and CQDs with Fe2+ ions at 2 mM each under 345 nm UV light.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs in the presence of various concentration of Fe2+ (from top to bottom, 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 4, 5, and 10 mM; λex at 280 nm). (b) A linear correlation between FL response and concentration of Fe2+ ion. (c) A linear correlation between FL response and concentration of Fe2+ ion.
quenching effect of Fe 2+ towards NCQDs-1.25 %.
quenching effect of Fe 2+ towards NCQDs-1.25 %.
Table of comparison between various biomass-derived CQDs used as fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Optimized nitrogen-doping of carbon quantum dots from Banana Peel waste: A highly selective Fe2+ sensor probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence at: Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research explores into the eco-friendly synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) utilizing discarded banana peel waste as a sustainable carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen dopant. The study aims on the critical gap in environmentally sustainable ion detection system by optimizing the fluorescent properties of CQDs for Fe2+ detection applications through nitrogen doping at an optimum ratio. Herein, we reported a facile one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize NCQDs with varying nitrogen dopant concentrations. The precursor material was characterized by thermal gravimetric analysis and Raman spectroscopy which exhibited a more amorphous carbon structure. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and Proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy proves the successful bonding of nitrogen functional group to pristine CQDs. The obtained nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) exhibited precise size control, with an average size of 5.05 nm. Optical analysis showed that NCQDs with a nitrogen doping level of 1.25 % had the best photostability, brightness, and fluorescence stability. These NCQDs demonstrated high selectivity in detecting Fe2+ through a fluorescent quenching mechanism. These findings suggested that obtained NCQDs has potential as environmentally friendly fluorescence probes in the application water filtration systems and medical diagnostics, particularly in Fe2+ detection and sensing due to their high selectivity and efficient fluorescent properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a unique zero-dimensional nanomaterial, was first discovered in 2004, and the term was then coined in 2006 [1]. CQDs has been seen as a rising star ever since due to its abundant, inexpensive, and environmentally-friendly nature [2]. In view of this, CQDs find widespread application in the application of optoelectronics, biological imaging, and ion probe scenarios owing to their low toxicity, exceptional optical characteristics and facile synthesis [3]. Fluorescence probes based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a highly promising area in biomedical imaging, and sensing. The mechanism of fluorescence probe works commonly by fluorescence quenching [1,4]. The mechanism involved is called photoinduced electron transfer (PET), which utilizes rapid electron transfer between electron acceptor and CQDs. This leads to nonradioative electron/hole recombination [5]. Generally, CQDs has been known for the detection of metal ions and among all the metal ion, Fe2+ is crucial in many chemical and biological processes [6]. In the aspect of water filtration application, ferrious iron (Fe2+ ) is the dominant form of iron in the underground, which converts to ferric iron (Fe3+) which is then filtered easily.Herein, CQDs can play the role of fluorescence sensor to ensure the full removal of iron particles [7]. Besides, in the medical field, Fe2+ plays an important role in oxygen transport, enzymatic reaction and even the mitrochondrial respiratory chain. However, excessive accumalation of Fe2+ causes serious health complications. Thus, accurate fluorescence detection could aid in the field of medicine [8].

Over the past decade, various synthesis methodologies have been developed including laser ablation, microwave method and even arc-discharge. However, this research paper focuses on hydrothermal due to its low cost, simple procedure, controllability of particle size and most importantly environmental friendliness [9,10].

The selection of precursor materials is a fundamental bedrock in the synthesis process of CQDs. Various carbon-rich sources, including biomass, polymers, and organic materials, are considered. The distinctive chemical compositions of the precursor materials affect the quantum dot's size, morphology, surface chemistry, and optical properties. In line with the goal of using sustainable precursor materials, researchers have focused on biomass waste due to its non-toxic nature, cost effectiveness, and feasibility of large-scale production [11]. The utilization of banana peel as the precursor material is motivated by its cost-effectiveness, excellent fluorescence stability, and easily tunable nature, as reported by Atchudan et al., (2021). It is proven that banana peel waste was found to be one of the highest components in the food waste composition generally in a typical Malaysian village [13]. Such biomass-waste ends up in the landfill and rots, which directly releases large amount of methane to the surrounding area [14]. An estimation of 77.6 Gg of methane (CH4) was released by the nothern region of Malaysia (Pulau Penang) alone [15]. Herein, the focus on reusing banana peel waste has a positive impact of reducing biomass-waste.

However, the synthesized CQDs has its limitations in the aspect of photostability (Miao et al. 2017). CQDs tends to agglomerate, which causes the particle size to increase and this is reflected as redshift of light emmission. This effect causes CQDs to reduce quantum effeciency over the time [11]. Herein, various modification routes were discovered to overcome the CQDs limitation, such as surface passivation, heretatom doping, composite and core-shell architecture modification [12]. However, this research focuses on heretoatom doping of nitrogen as the modification route which was reported to enhance the PL emission (up to 80 %) by John et al., (2021). The chosen modification route is based on the high electronegativity nature of nitrogen which enhances electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity. This enhancement contributes to the application of ion detection [2]. The final outcome of the modification is nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [13].

With the drive for sustainable materials, this research focuses on finding the optimum amount nitrogen source needed to be doped into banana peel waste derived CQDs for the application of Fe2+ detection.

Carbon precursor was prepared in the lab from banana peel waste (musa acuminata colla) which generally is discarded in the food and beverage industries. Ethylenediamine (≥99 % Aldrich, USA) was used as Nitrogen precursor source. All metal salts (NaCl, FeCl3, FeCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, Cr(NO3)3, SnCl2, and AgCl) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. All chemicals were analytical grade and only Ultrapure water was used in the experiments.

Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (NCQDs) and Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal strategy shown as Fig. 1 . Banana peel was dried at 80 °C overnight to remove as much moisture as possible and then ground into fine powder. The fine banana peel powder is used as the carbon source for CQDs. To determine the most optimum fluorescent character of NCQDs, the ethylenediamine (nitrogen-source) concentration was varied. This is to study further the relationship between ratio of nitrogen doping and the optical properties of NCQDs. The percentage of ethylenediamine added onto the mixture were (1.25, 5, 10 and 15 %). The strategy involved mixing prepared carbon precursor of 0.06 g and varied amount of ethylenediamine in 80 ml of water, followed by hydrothermal process in teflon lined stainless steel autoclave at 185 °C for 24 h. Brown solution was obtained and it is then filtered with 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove the residue. The final product (NCQDs-1.25 %, NCQDs-5 %, NCQDs-10 %, and NCQDs-15 %) should be yellowish tinted clear solution. The CQDs were prepared via the same route without adding any nitrogen-source into the mixture before hydrothermal. The prepared CQDs is used as a comparative towards optimized NCQDs at the aspect of fluorescence properties.

The optimized NCQDs was then used to detect the metal ion selectivity test. Various metal ion salt (NaCl, FeCl3, FeCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, Cr(NO3)3, SnCl2, and AgCl) was prepared at the concentration of 2 mM and mixed with the ratio of 1:10 which is 1 part metal ion solution and 10 parts of NCQDs (optimized). The mixture is then mixed for 5 min before undergoing fluorescence emission testing.

Thermogravimetric analysis was done via TGA analyzer (TGA-50, Shimadzu, Japan). TGA analysis was used to characterize the thermal stability at the heating rate at 10 °C/min from 30 to 600 °C. Taloc L120C transmission electron microscope (FEI, Holland) with an acceleration voltage of 120 kV was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were measured by Spectrum 400 FT-IR (Perkin Elmer, USA) at the range of 650 to 4000 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was deployed by Axis Ultra DLD (Kratos, United Kingdom) to investigate the surface elemental and futher provide types of the bonding present. The proton nuclear magnetic resonance (H1NMR) spectra was recorded with Avance III HD 400 MHz (Bruker, USA) using D2O as solvent. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra via Lambda 950 (Perkin Elmer, USA) were recorded at the range of 200 to 700 nm. The fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) was measured by using F7000 (Hitachi, Japan) at emmission scanning mode (range: 280–600 nm). The fluorescence decay profile was measured by using FLS920 (Edinburgh, United Kingdom). To understand the zeta potential and size distribution, dynamic light scattering measurement (DLS) was executed via ZEN 3600 (Malvern,UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shape, size and functional group characterization of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: This research paper focuses on optimizing the nitrogen ratio to produce NCQDs that suits the application of fluorescence probe. The optimized NCQDs particle size and functional group is analysed via TEM and FTIR Besides, H1NMR and XPS were also deployed to further provide prove of further successful doping of the Nitrogen element. The TEM exhibited in Fig. 4 clearly shows the average particle size of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 % are 6.25 nm and 5.05 nm. It is believed that CQDs has gone through agglomeration over time. The NCQDs-1.25 % showcases a more stable and consistent [20,21].

To further understand the characterization of NCQDs compared to pristine CQDs, the FTIR spectrum was characterized as shown in Fig. 5 [22]. The FTIR result of NCQDs-1.25 % was compared to CQDs to understand the difference in the surface functional group that nitrogen doping affected pristine CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5, a broad strong band in the range of 3090–3550 cm−1 was observed in both CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 %. This peak is due to the stretching vibration of OH bond which represents that there is presence of -OH group on the surface [23]. The appearance of two bands at 2100, 1630 and 1145 cm−1 exhibit the existence of stretching vibration for CC (sp2-hybridized carbon), CO and CO respectively which proves the existence of carbonyl group matching UV–Vis peaks is essential to obtain a core-shell structure of CQDs [24,25]. Next, the two peak at 1544 and 1266 cm−1 represent NH and CN vibration respectively. These peaks are only detectable on NCQDs-1.25 % which directly proves the doping of Nitrogen towards CQDs and the presence of amine group on NCQDs [17].

To further support the chemical structure and bonding finding above, H1NMR spectroscopy has been deployed on synthesized CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 % (Fig. 6 ). Focusing on the CQDs as visualized on Fig. 6 (a), the chemical shift (δ) of 8.3 ppm indicates proton bonding to aromatic chain [26]. The chemical shift (δ) of 4.7 and 2.4 ppm reveals the presence of proton bonded to CO and CO, respectively. The following result is able to support the finding of FTIR spectra thus proving the existence of the carbonyl group. Shifting the focus towards NCQDs-1.25 % as presented on Fig. 6 (b), the chemical shift peaks are identical with those of CQDs except for two noticeable differences. The first difference is related to the sharp amine group peak at 2.6 ppm which supports the idea of successful nitrogen doping [27]. The second difference is that the peak of 2.4 ppm is less intense in NCQDs-1.25 % compared to CQDs. This does explain the reduction of carbonylic group on the CQDs as it is replaced with amine group.

To prove that nitrogen doping is successful at the surface of carbon quantum dots, XPS analysis was initiated to identify the surface functional groups on the NCQDs [28]. The acquired result of the XPS spectrum of NCQDs-1.25 %, shown in Fig. 7(a) , reveals that there are three predominant components at 284.84, 398.81 and 560.13 eV which correspond to the binding energies of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. These components confirm the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements on the surface of the NCQDs [28,29]. Further analysis of the high-resolution of C1s spectrum in Fig. 7(b) identified three components were identified which are CC (sp3), CN (sp3), and CO (sp2) at 284.20 eV, 287.84 eV and 280.37 eV respectively. On the other hand, The O1s spectrum in Fig. 7(c), two components were identified at 530.61 eV and 531.34 eV which corresponds to CO (sp2) and CO (sp2) species. Lastly on the N1S spectrum in Fig. 7(d), two components can be identified at 399.78 eV and 405.70 eV which corresponds to CN (sp3) and NH (sp2) species. These results clearly demonstrate the successful synthesis of NCQDs incorporating nitrogen, oxygen and carbon elements [29,30] .

The optical character is an important factor for a flouresence probe. In this case, finding the optimum ratio of nitrogen-source doped is crucial and following with that, this research focuses on comparing the optical characterization between CQDs, NCQDs-1.25 %, NCQDs-5 %, NCQDs-10 %, and NCQDs-15 %. Diving deep into the optical characteristics, UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) were deployed as represented in Fig. 8(a) represents UV–vis absorbance spectra. It is worth noting that Fig. 8(a) shows there are two typical absorption peaks for the NCQDs meanwhile only one absorption peak for CQDs. The first peak which is at 270 nm and 280 nm at NCQDs and CQDs respectively represents π-π* transition of graphite core (C=C) [31]. From the first peak, it is clearly visualized that nitrogen doping of various concentrations caused a clear blue-shifted absorbance peak compared to CQDs. Next, the second peak represents n-π* transition of CN on the surface of NCQDs which is why it is only found on NCQDs at 315 nm as the nitrogen-source initiated the surface modification with amino functional group adhered to the carbon core. Interestingly, increasing the concentration of Nitrogen exhibited a lesser obvious peak. NCQDs-1.25 % exhibit the most clear peak which translates to inefficiency of absorbing light. Based on Fig. 8(a), it is clearly stated that NCQDs-1.25 % exhibit the best in the aspect of light absorption.

Fig. 8(b) represents the FL spectra of CQDs, NCQDs-1.25 %, NCQDs-5 %, NCQDs-10 % and NCQDs-15 % at emission mode (range: 280–600 nm). The excitation parameter was set based on UV–vis absorbance peak and it indicated the light was most efficiently absorbed at 280 nm. It is clearly visualized that NCQDs-1.25 % was able to produce the highest FL intensity at the peak of 413 nm (emission wavelength). The FL emission of the NCQDs is gradually red-shifted as the concentration of nitrogen-source increases. This indicates that the larger particle size is correlated with an increasing in nitrogen source concentration pass the 1.25 % concentration [32]. Herein, the fluorescence emission intensity peak area of NCQDs-1.25 % is approximately 13 times larger than CQDs. NCQDs-15 % also shows an improvement of luminescence intensity up to 70 % compared to CQDs. On a side note, the fluorescence quantum yield of NCQDs-1.25 % was at 13.40 % by measuring using quinine sulphate as reference [33, 34]. NCQDs-1.25 % does not only have much larger PL intensity at the same concentration but it also has a more blue-shifted peak which indicates of lower non-radiative recombination and shorter stoke (refer to eq. 1) [13,35]. Stoke shift, named after Sir George Gabriel Stokes, is a phenomenon which represents the characteristics of excited photon redshifted from absorbed photon. Stoke shift occurs when the photon absorbed excites electrons to a higher energy state but then the electrons might undergo various relaxation processes which drops into a lower energy state before reacting to the ground state for radiative recombination [35]. The vibrational relaxation process is due to intramolecular vibration energy loss [36]. The equation of stoke shift is presented as below (1):
∆
λ stoke
= ∆
λ em
− ∆
λ ex
∆
λ stoke
of CQDs = 160
nm
(1)
∆
λ stoke
of NCQDs
1.25 %
= 133
nm

As stated, the stoke shift of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 % is at 160 and 133 nm, respectively which clearly states the lower vibrational energy loss of NCQDs-1.25 %. This CQDs has carbonyl group which are known for conjugated conjugated π-domains which facilitate energy transfer processes that causes non-radiative recombination. However, introducing nitrogen source, the carbonyl group was reduced by amine group via necleophilic reaction [37]. To generalize UV–Vis and FL result, NCQDs-1.25 % does not only outperform CQDs but also NCQDs-5 %, NCQDs-10 %, and NCQDs-15 %. NCQDs-1.25 % exhibited.

Fig. 9(a) represents the Tauc plot of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 % which is able to showcase the comparison of the energy band gap between the quantum particles. The Tauc plot displays (αhν)2 vs. hv, where α, h, and ν are absorption coefficients and 2 is the power factor used as a fit for the set of data [38]. As we can observe CQDs has a linearity at the energy level of 4.33 eV and the NCQDs-1.25 % has 2 linearities at 4.71 and 2.83 eV. Not only that NCQDs-1.25 % has 2 linearities but it also has its energy band gap at a higher value. The two linearities represent the two peak of UV–Vis spectra which just means the presence of two electronic transitions (core and functionalized surface). With the given optical analysis results and Tauc plot, it is suggested that the energy band gap in CQDs was successfully increased from 4.33 to 4.71 eV with the dopant of nitrogen-source, as shown in Fig. 9(b) [39].

Fig. 10 visualize the fluorescence decay profile of CQDs and the most optimum NCQDs which is NCQDs-1.25 %. The decay profile was intiated to understand the dynamic of quantum quenching of CQDs once surface functionalized with nitrogen doping. The average life time,
τ avg
was calculated with a Time-Resolved Photoluminescencas (TRPL) formula below (2) [35]:
(2)
τ avg
=
τ 1
2
B 1
+
τ 2
2
B 2
+
τ 3
2
B 3
B 1
+
B 2
+
B 3
Where τ
1, τ
2 and τ
3 represents the decay lifetime and B1, B2 and B3 is the corresponding weightage. The average life-time shifted from 2.1 to 5.4 ns comparing CQDs to NCQDs. This result exhibited that NCQDs-1.25 % has much slower decay [13,40]. Thus, nitrogen source of the concentration of 1.25 % successfully reduced the quantum quenching compared to CQDs.

DLS measurement was deployed to compare the optical stability of the most optimum nitrogen-source does aligns with the idea of lower agglomeration effect. The FL spectra of NCQDs-1.25 % also represented a much higher intensity compared to CQDs and this is because of the fact that lower non-radiative recombination. As indicated by FTIR spectra, pristine concentration, NCQDs-1.25 %, with that of to pristine CQDs. Zeta potential and size distribution was plotted in Fig. 11
(a-b). In optical characteristics of QDs, the stability of the colloidals plays an important role. The reduction of aggregation will ensure that the fluorescence intensity will not deplete over a period of time. Based on Fig. 11(a), the average size of CQDs and NCQDs-1.25 % is 7.762 nm (std. dev: 3.88 nm) and 5.511 nm (std. dev: 0.71 nm). Do note that the sizing of DLS is based on hydrodynamic which is the apparent particle size as it interacts with the surrounding solvent [41,42]. It is proven that NCQDs-1.25 % holds a more stable size control compared to CQDs as the standard deviation much smaller. This showcases that nitrogen doping improves radiative recombination hence enhances the fluorescence properties of CQDs [43]. This statement is backed by the FL spectra result of Fig. 8(b). Referring to Fig. 11(b), the zeta potential shifted from −15.2 mV to −26.8 mV once the CQDs is doped with nitrogen. As observed, the zeta potential value dives deeper towards the negative region, this signifies that the electrostatic forces are even more repulsive [44]. This indicates that the surface of NCQDs-1.25 % have negative charge moieties [41]. Generally, the higher negative value of zeta potential showcases that NCQDs-1.25 % is more stable than CQDs which will greatly benefits the optical properties as the more repulsive particle prevents agglomeration due to repulsion of -ve particle charge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CQDs towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: With the momentum of understanding the selective detection of various metal ions, Na+, Sn 4+, Fe 3+, Fe 2+, Cu 2+, Cr 6+, Ca 2+ and Ag+ were mixed with NCQDs-1.25 %. The mixed solutions are then gone through FL testing to obtain the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 12(a) represents the relative fluorescence intensity (I/Io) of NCQDs-1.25 % with the presence of various metal ions (2 mM) during the excitation wavelength of 280 nm. As observed, the Fe 2+ exhibits the lower fluorescence intensity which represents fluorescence quenching of almost 67 %. The rest metal ions only exhibited <20 %. This is related to the strong coordination interaction of Fe 2+ with the surface functional group of NCQDs-1.25 % which was highly decorated with amine group [45]. Fig. 13(a) illustrates a comparison between NCQDs-1.25 % and pristine CQDs. The results indicate that pristine CQDs does exhibit a little fluorescence quenching effect in the presence of Fe 2+ but not in a scale of observable difference, whereas NCQDs-1.25 % show significant fluorescence quenching under the same conditions. In the effort to study the quenching effect of Fe 2+, various concentration of Fe 2+ was added to the NCQDs and the fluorescence emission intensity was measured and visualized in Fig. 14 (a). The concentration of Fe 2+ was varied from 0.1 to 10 mM.

Fig. 14(b) shows the relationship between the concentration of Fe 2+ and FL intensity at the range of 0.1 to 10 mM. Importantly, there is a good linear correlation which deduced the fitting equation below (3):
(3)
F 0
− F
F 0
= 0.19404
Fe
2 +
+ 0.06113

[FL] and [Fe 2+] represents the fluorescence intensity. The fitted linear line has a correlation index (R2) of 0.9771. With the given information, the detection limit for Fe 2+ was calculated with 3.3(σ/m) where σ denotes as the standard deviation and m denotes as the gradient of the linear line [46]. Based on Fig. 12(b), the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.19 mM. Based on Fig. 14 (b), NCQDs-1.25 % demonstrated a linear relationship with Fe 2+ at the range between 0.3 and 4.0 mM. This result showcased that the synthesized NCQDs-1.25 % exhibit an acceptable selectivity and sensitivity performance as a “on-off” fluorescent probe. The performance of the NCQDs-1.25 % is compared to other studies and this comparison is tabulated in Table 1 . This study uniquely demonstrates the use of banana peel as a precursor material for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for the detection of Fe 2+ ions. The usage of banana peel has been highly regarded as a sustainable material as Malaysians second highest food-waste.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of NCQDs-1.25 % detecting Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the possible quenching mechanism of NCQDs-1.25 % detecting Fe 2+, the fluorescence lifetime has been deployed to compare the NCQDs-1.25 % effect with the presence of Fe 2+ ion. According to the Fig. 15 , It can be observed that the fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs-1.25 % has reduced from 5.4 ns to 3.1 ns. This is possible result of the dynamic quenching mechanism which further explains the formation of complex coordination between the oxygen functional group of the NCQDs surface with Fe 2+ ion [47]. Due to the chelation, energy excited towards the NCQDs has been transfer to the Fe 2+ which acts as a dynamic quencher [48]. This mechanism is further explained visually on Fig. 16 .

As visualized on Fig. 16, the quenching effect of Fe 2+ towards NCQDs-1.25 % is via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) whereas the excited electrons from NCQDs-1.25 % is transferred to the metal ion to from a bonding. This eventually led to nonradiative recombination [13]. Based on the research done by Phan et al., 2022, it was proven that the formation of FeO bonds on the surface of NCQDs existed which explains the PET and led to fluorescence quenching.

In conclusion, the study addresses a critical gap in environmentally sustainable ion detection systems by optimizing the fluorescent properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for Fe2+ detection applications through nitrogen doping at an optimum ratio, while also demonstrating the successful bonding of nitrogen functional groups to pristine CQDs. The obtained NCQDs demonstrated superior size control with an average size of 5.05 nm as well as good quantum yield at 13.40 %. Optical analysis revealed that obtained NCQDs-1.25 % exhibited the most optimal photostability, brightness, and fluorescence stability which are suitable for the fluorescent probe applications. The deployment of NCQDs-1.25 % as a fluorescent probe for Fe2+ detection through a fluorescent quenching mechanism resulted in high selectivity. These findings underscore the potential of NCQDs synthesized from banana peel waste as a promising eco-friendly material for Fe2+ sensing applications, particularly in the application of water filtration and medical diagnosis. In terms of water filtration, the usage of obtained NCQDs could be implemented to detect the conversion of Fe2+ to Fe3+. On the other hand, in medical diagnosis, NCQDs has potential in the application of detection of iron homeostasis process in the mitochondria [54].
This contribution is original; the work has not been published previously under other publications and is not currently under evaluation by another journal.
This research has been funded by the Research University Grant (GUP-2022-045) from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia.
Harivalagan Siva Kumar:[writing-original draft, visualization, methodology, investigation conceptualization, data curation, formal analysis]
Siti Aisyah Shamsudin:[writing-review & editing, validation, supervision, funding acquisition, resources, project administration]
Muhammad Nasrullah Bin Ahmad Azian:[Formal analysis, methodology, Investigation]

Harivalagan Siva Kumar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Siti Aisyah Shamsudin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Muhammad Nasrullah Bin Ahmad Azian: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Siti Aisyah Shamsudin reports financial support and equipment, chemicals, or supplies were provided by Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (GUP-2022-045). The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
